                                                                    Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391


                                                                       Contents lists available at ScienceDirect


                                                               Egyptian Informatics Journal
                                                                   journal homepage: www.sciencedirect.com


Full length article

Diagnosis of patellofemoral osteoarthritis using enhanced sequential deep
learning techniques
Mai Ramadan Ibraheem a, Saleh Naif Almuayqil b, A. A. Abd El-Aziz b, c, Medhat A. Tawfeek d, e, *,
Fatma M. Talaat f
a
  Dept. Information Technology, Faculty of Computers and Information, Kafrelsheikh University, Egypt
b
  Dept. of Information Systems, College of Computer and Information Sciences, Jouf University, Saudi Arabia (KSA)
c
  Dept. of Information Systems and Technology, Faculty of Graduate Studies for Statistical Research, Cairo University, Egypt
d
  Dept. of Computer Science, College of Computer and Information Sciences, Jouf University, Saudi Arabia (KSA)
e
  Dept. of Computer Science, Faculty of Computers and Information, Menoufia University, Egypt
f
  Dept. Machine Learning & Information Retrieval Department, Faculty of Artificial Intelligence, Kafrelsheikh University, Egypt




A R T I C L E I N F O                                        A B S T R A C T

Keywords:                                                    The surface electromyography (sEMG) signal is a complex interference pattern resulting from the electrical
SEMG Signals                                                 activity of contracting muscles, which directly correlates with muscle activity and exercise level. Patellofemoral
Data Augmentation                                            osteoarthritis (PF OA) refers to the softening and destruction of the articular cartilage of the knee cap, which is
Time Series Analysis
                                                             important to diagnose in the early phase. The aim of this study is to develop a predictive model, called the
Lower Limbs
                                                             Enhanced Sequencing Deep Learning (PESDL) Algorithm, to detect PF OA from the sEMG signal. The algorithm
Sequence Data
                                                             consists of four main modules: (i) Data Acquisition Module (DAM), (ii) Signal Preprocessing Module (SPM), (iii)
                                                             Data Augmentation and Concatenation Module (DACM), and (iv) Lower Limb Classification (LLC). The sEMG
                                                             signals from five core muscles of the lower limb were acquired during stepping stairs up and down, and machine
                                                             learning was used to obtain the muscle activation data signals. The acquired data was preprocessed, augmented,
                                                             concatenated, and shuffled. Three feedforward deep networks (RNN, LSTM, and GRU) were used to classify the
                                                             lower limb sEMG time-sequence data, with the GRU network showing better performance than the other two.
                                                             The model’s performance was evaluated using seven performance measures and 10-fold cross-validation. The
                                                             maximum values of feedforward deep networks (RNN), LSTM, and GRU for the five muscles (RF, BF, VM, ST, and
                                                             FX) using seven performance measures were as follows: RNN (0.961, 0.154, 0.943, 0.244, 1.0, 1.0, 0.899), LSTM
                                                             (0.967, 0.147, 0.945, 0.217, 0.997, 1.0, 0.927), and GRU (0.976, 0.111, 0.949, 0.212, 0.996, 0.998, 0.938),
                                                             respectively, listed in the order of accuracy, loss, validation accuracy, validation loss, recall, precision, and F1-
                                                             score. Comparisons with other state-of-the-art models using the same datasets demonstrated the effective per­
                                                             formance of the predictive model. The results suggest that RNN models, particularly GRU and LSTM, can be
                                                             effective for detecting PF OA, and the specific RNN architecture used can have a significant impact on
                                                             performance.




1. Introduction                                                                                     1.1. Patellofemoral osteoarthritis (PF OA)

   This section discusses some important issues, such as Patellofemoral                                 Patellofemoral osteoarthritis (PF OA) is the softening and damage of
osteoarthritis (PF OA), Long-Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Gated                                        the articular cartilage of the knee cap, which is the cartilage lining
Recurrent Unit (GRU), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), and the pri­                                 beneath the knee cap [1]. Progressive rehabilitation activities are
mary contribution of this study.                                                                    necessary for PF OA sufferers to regain full muscle function, as insta­
                                                                                                    bility or reduced core strength may contribute to the increased risk of
                                                                                                    lower extremity injuries [2]. Since the stability of the trunk and pelvis is
                                                                                                    essential for limb movement, surface electromyographies (sEMG) are

1.2. Recurrent neural networks (RNN)                                              f t = σ (Wf • [ht− 1 , xt ] + bf )                                              (2b)

    Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are networks that have feedback              ot = σ (Wo • [ht− 1 , xt ] + bo )                                               (2c)
connections between neurons and are capable of handling sequential
data. In RNNs, the output of the previous step is used as input for the           ct = tanh (Wc • [ht− 1 , xt ] + bc )                                            (2d)
current step. RNNs have a memory that keeps track of all the informa­
tion that has been processed. Since the network performs the same                 ct = f t • ct− 1 + it • ct                                                      (2e)
operation on all inputs or hidden layers to produce an output, it uses the
same parameters for each input, which simplifies the model’s parame­              ht = ot • tanh (ct )                                                             (2f)
ters compared to other neural networks. RNNs use a hidden layer to                    The activation of LSTM cells given by Equation 2 shows the
address this issue. The hidden state, which stores a portion of a se­             requirement for previous knowledge in time. Thus, the network
quence’s information, is the most significant and essential feature of            parameter optimization via stochastic gradient descent is performed
RNNs [4]. Fig. 1 illustrates an RNN. The general form of the network can          through the time steps in the order of the input data. Correspondingly,
be expressed by Equation (1), where X ∈ Rt×d represents the input data            the data input is split into smaller windows to decrease the time depth of
with t steps and d channels. The output X ∈ Rt×nc has dimensionality nc           BP backpropagation. Typically, in an FFNN structure, the number of
and the same length, t. Θ denotes the parameters of the network and L             variables and time duration of the input layer are fixed. There are
is a one-to-one correspondence in which each time step corresponds to             several hidden layers in fully connected LSTM layers, and an output
an output time steps [3].                                                         layer serves as a classification softmax transfer function. The input and

    A fundamental limitation of RNNs is their difficulty in processing            states consequently lasted for consecutive training sequences [11].
long sequences due to limited access to input information [3]. RNNs
have access to less input information; they only consider the first few
features rather than entire sequences. As a result, RNN isn’t as good as          1.4. Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
other recurrent layers with more complex layers at digesting long se­
quences [5]. In section 3, we will discuss the overall steps in RNN to                Although GRU may not have as much representational power as
enhance its performance.                                                          LSTM, it operates on the same premises and is more streamlined,
                                                                                  resulting in lower operating costs. To address the issue with ordinary
1.3. Long Short-Term memory (LSTM)                                                RNN, GRU integrates two gate operating mechanisms called the update
                                                                                  gate and the reset gate [12]. The update gate is responsible for figuring
    LSTM solves the problem of vanishing gradients by adding the ability          out the value of prior data that is required for the next cell. It is powerful
to transfer data across many timesteps. Input data sequences can be               for the model to copy all the previous data to minimize the risk of
loaded at any time, carried to a later timestep, and retrieved when               vanishing gradients. The reset gate is responsible for deciding how much
needed. The information is preserved for later use, and older signals are         of the previous information can be discarded [13].
also prevented from being lost within the process [6]. Learning long-                 The update gate shown by Eq. (3a) has a single weight to customize
term relationships in RNN has been found as a limitation issue. LSTM              the influence of the prior hidden state ht− 1 on the updated. Contrary to
consecutively addresses this issue by enriching the hidden node pa­               the reset gate given by Eq. (3b), which determines the number of past
rameters to overcome looping problems and customize the acquiring                 states that are discarded, the memory cells updates ht are shown by Eq.
and releasing of states based on the input sequence [6]. Thus, the states
                                                                                  (3c). The output is a computed using the updated ht state and the prior
can be activated based on the short-term episode while holding the
                                                                                  h(t-1) state, as determined by Eq. (3d). With fewer parameters, GRU
network states active, providing the result of long-term memory. RNN is
                                                                                  almost has the advantages of LSTMs over RNNs [14]. Therefore, GRU
not as good as LSTM at learning the sequences [7]. LSTM has four
weight/bias pairs rather than RNN, which has a bias and a single weight.
The four weight/bias pairs are as follows [8]: (i) Forget Gate Layer, (ii)
Input Gate Layer, (iii) Output Gate Layer, (iv) State Gate Layer.

cusses related work. Section 3 presents the proposed framework phases,
                                                                                  intrinsic joint properties from a temporal perspective, utilising LSTM
and section 4 shows figures, the used datasets, and the experimental

results. Finally, the discussion and conclusion are explained in Section 5.

2. Literature review                                                              Additionally, patient sEMG data may not be comprehensive and should
                                                                                  be thought of as adapting to various walking environments.
    Several attempts have been made to address the difficulties

exoskeleton control. During normal adult walking, the sEMG and the                 dicted results. Further, the proposed model performs preprocessing,

principal component analysis (RFPCA) was used to establish the model               Different fine-tuning settings have also been set to increase classification
for knee movement in sEMG.                                                         performance for knee abnormalities. The proposed novel deep classifi­
    The estimated model RFPCA was compared to a back propagation                   cation model for sEMG sequential data has reported significant results.
neural network with principal component analysis to validate their                     The previous algorithms used for detecting PF OA using sEMG signals
findings (BPPCA). RFPCA was found appropriate for all experiment                   have some limitations includeing:
participants and takes less time to execute. RFPCA had a mean square
error of about 5, which was lower than BPPCA, which had errors ranging             1. Limited feature extraction and selection methods: Previous algo­
from 7 to 25. The researchers concluded that the RFPCA approach for                   rithms used basic time and frequency domain features, such as mean,
estimating knee movement from sEMG is practical and might be                          variance, and power spectral density, which may not capture the full
employed for exoskeleton control and motion analysis.                                 complexity of sEMG signals.
    Tsinganos et al. [20] used two datasets to evaluate existing data              2. Limited data augmentation techniques: Previous algorithms used
augmentation methodologies for electromyography signals (i.e.,                        simple data augmentation techniques, such as random noise and
magnitude warping, overlapping windows, wavelet decomposition,                        time-shifting, which may not effectively enhance the diversity and
synthetic sEMG models, and additive noise). The AtzoriNet, a modified                 variability of the sEMG data.
version of a lightweight CNN for gesture recognition, was used in the              3. Limited deep learning architectures: Previous algorithms used only a
test. A collection of metrics and visualizations (such as classification              few deep learning architectures, such as convolutional neural net­
accuracy, the davies–Bouldin index, and the silhouette score) aid in                  works (CNNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs), and long short-
evaluating and offering information on their performance. Over the two                term memory (LSTM) networks, which may not fully exploit the
benchmark datasets, signal magnitude warping and wavelet decompo­                     temporal dependencies and sequential nature of sEMG signals.
sition significantly boost classification accuracy (up to 16%).
    Morbidoni et al. [21] proposed a deep learning approach for sEMG-                  The proposed PESDL algorithm addresses these limitations by
based classification and prediction of the foot-floor contact signal in            incorporating novel features, such as higher-order statistics and wavelet
different natural walking conditions. In [21], 23 healthy individuals              transforms, advanced data augmentation techniques, such as time-series
were asked to record sEMG signals from eight lower-limb muscles while              data generation and concatenation, and a combination of deep learning
walking on flat ground, following an eight-shaped pattern that included            architectures, including RNNs, LSTMs, and gated recurrent units
natural acceleration, a deceleration curve, and reversing. They                    (GRUs).
concluded that utilizing a multilayer perceptron to learn hidden features              The contributions of the proposed algorithm can be summarized as
gave acceptable performance while avoiding feature engineering                     follows:
through a comprehensive evaluation. The results showed an average
categorization accuracy of 94.9 for subjects who had received training             1. Enhanced feature extraction and selection: The PESDL algorithm
and 93.4 for untrained people, with heel-strike and toe-strike having                 incorporates novel higher-order statistical and wavelet-based fea­
mean absolute differences of 21.6 ms and 38.1 ms, respectively, between               tures that capture the non-Gaussian and non-stationary nature of
phase transition timing forecasts and footswitch data.                                sEMG signals more accurately than previous algorithms.
    Khowailed and Abotabl [22] introduced the neural muscular acti­                2. Improved data augmentation techniques: The PESDL algorithm uses
vation detection (NMAD) framework, which uses deep learning to                        more advanced time-series data generation and concatenation
identify muscle activation. Not only does the proposed method greatly                 techniques that enhance the diversity and variability of the sEMG
enhance the time detection accuracy, but it can also adapt to varying                 data more effectively than previous algorithms.
levels of interference and signal-to-noise ratio due to its training nature.       3. A combination of advanced deep learning architectures: The PESDL
The key notion is that, rather than relying exclusively on heuristic as­              algorithm combines three different deep learning architectures
pects of the sEMG signal to predict activation timing, an artificial neural           (RNNs, LSTMs, and GRUs) to fully exploit the temporal dependencies
network can be trained to do so. Because the sEMG signal is time series, a            and sequential nature of sEMG signals and achieve higher classifi­
recurrent neural network (RNN) is a good candidate for determining the                cation accuracy than previous algorithms.
timing of muscle activity. The findings suggest that a properly trained
RNN can outperform state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of accuracy.                   Overall, the proposed PESDL algorithm addresses some of the limi­
Furthermore, the trained neural network can work reliably in a wide                tations of previous algorithms used for detecting PF OA using sEMG
range of SNR and cross-talk conditions.                                            signals and contributes to the research gap by providing a more accurate
    In evaluating sEMG quality, Akhundov et al. [23] assessed the per­             and reliable method for early detection of PF OA, which could help to
formance of supervised and unsupervised artificial neural networks                 improve clinical outcomes and patient quality of life.
(ANNs). The ANNs were trained (n = 28,000), tested (n = 12,000), and                   The proposed Enhanced Sequencing Deep Learning (PESDL) Algo­
evaluated (n = 47,000) in classifying signals into four categories. When           rithm contains four main modules, including the Lower Limb Classifi­
compared to supervised ANNs, unsupervised ANNs showed a 30–40%                     cation (LLC) module, which is responsible for improving the
improvement in classification accuracy (>98%). AlexNet had the best                performance of deep neural networks. Specifically, the LLC module
accuracy (99.55%) and the fewest false classifications.                            utilizes three types of recurrent neural networks (RNN), including RNN,

3. Proposed Enhanced Sequencing Deep Learning (PESDL)                         the negative effects from achieving a timely and accurate interpretation
                                                                              of PF OA.
    The following is the main contribution of this paper. The proposed
Enhanced Sequencing Deep Learning (PESDL) Algorithm contains four             3.1. Data Acquisition Module (DAM)
main modules, as shown in Fig. 4, which are; (i) Data Acquisition
Module (DAM), (ii) Signal Preprocessing Module (SPM), (iii) Data                 This database contains 22 male samples, 11 with knee abnormalities
Augmentation and Concatenation Module (DACM)., and (iv) Lower                 and 11 with healthy people. We used electromyography and goniometry
Limb Classification (LLC).                                                    equipment from MWX8 Datalog Biometrics to acquire these data. We
    In DAM, the sEMG signals were acquired from five core muscles of          used a goniometer for the knee and four electrodes (Vastus Medialis,
the lower limb in about 200 reads from healthy and patient adults             Semitendinosus, Biceps Femoris, and Rectus Femoris). Fig. 5 shows
during stepping up and down stairs. The five core muscles, TrA, VMO,          samples of the acquired data for one of the five muscles of interest RF in
GM, VL, and ML, were acquired to obtain the muscle activation data            normal people. Fig. 6 shows the samples for muscle FX in abnormal
signal. The acquired data is then preprocessed, augmented, concate­           people.
nated, and shuffled. The augmentation step was performed using a time-
series generator yielding samples and their targets, resulting in 1056        3.2. Signal preprocessing Module (SPM)
records for the five muscles of interest. Three feedforward deep net­
works are selected to represent and classify the lower limb sEMG time-           The quality of the sEMG signal captured is affected by the quantity,
sequence data. RNN, LSTM, and GRU are used in the classification of the       depth, tissue content, and location of the activated fibres in the muscle.
time-sequence data because they can control the input flow and prevent        These variables can mask undesirable signals or cancel out the signal

                                                                          5
M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                                 Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391


                                                                                 3.3. Data augmentation and Concatenation Module (DACM)

                                                                                     This module combines two submodules; (i) Data Augmentation and
                                                                                 (ii) Data Concatenation and Shuffle.

                                                                                 3.3.1. Data augmentation
                                                                                     Data augmentation is a set of methods for increasing the amount and
                                                                                 variety of data available for training models for a certain job without
                                                                                 acquiring additional data. This group of strategies helps the model learn
                                                                                 the extent of intraclass variation that can be seen by changing existing
                                                                                 labelled samples into synthetic or artificial data [27]. Generating addi­
                                                                                 tional data while keeping the correct label is one of the challenging parts
                                                                                 of data augmentation. Numerous tasks require domain expertise, which
                                                                                 may be challenging to acquire in the case of sEMG due to significant
                                                                                 within-subject variability. In other words, the same person can execute
                                                                                 the same gesture in different ways, and the quality of the recorded signal
                                                                                 is influenced by things like fatigue and sweat [28]). Because enhanced
                                                                                 data are supposed to reflect a larger set of possible data points, data
                                                                                 augmentation is one of the techniques for dealing with the problem of
                                                                                 overfitting. It reduces the difference in the training, validation, and
                                                                                 testing sets [29].
                                                                                     Augmentation approaches for biosignal processing have been
                                                                                 created as a series of simple alterations in sequence (e.g., shuffling,
                                                                                 warping, additive noise, or time). In addition, the authors have devised
                                                                                 alternative approaches for generating augmented data based on sub-
                       Fig. 5. Normal samples for RF muscle.                     optimally matched sequences [30]. Compared to previous approaches,
                                                                                 their methodology performed better or equivalently across various
from the muscle we’re trying to record. The absolute value of the signal         datasets, including ECG and EEG signals. Several sEMG-based gesture
is used to apply full-wave rectification. Rectification is the process of        recognition algorithms have been tested using a set of domain-specific
transforming a negative swing into a positive swing. Fig. 7 shows the            augmen,tations. Sensor noise simulation, electrode displacement simu­
raw samples of the data before preprocessing.                                    lation, and muscle fatigue approximation are among them [20].
    Rectification is accomplished by smoothing the signal by high-                   This study generated enhanced data using a sliding window tech­
passing it. High-pass filtering effectively eliminates the effects of            nique and non-overlapping signal segments. The augmentation study
maintaining a constant force or of repeated fluctuations in the strength         reveals that using a sliding window technique improves performance
of the sEMG signals [25]. A high-pass filter works effectively to remove         significantly [31]. Data augmentation is used to create a newly labelled
the oscillation produced by overlaid artfacts. By using a design function        signal x*i, y*i from an existing one x i, y i without changing the class
of order 4, a pass band frequency of 75 kHz, and a sampling frequency of         label, i.e., y i = y*i. Data augmentation is achieved in this study by
1000 Hz, this step builds a high pass filter [26]. The developed high-pass       smoothing the signal of sEMG sequences using the Root-Mean-Squared
filter efficiently converts signals in the time domain with variable res­        method (RMS) [31]. Convolution is also used to improve the looping
olution. The algorithm for the preprocessing step is given in Algorithm          of the window movement, as shown in Fig. 9.
1. The output of the rectification step is shown in Fig. 8.                          In addition to considering the signal’s time- and frequency-domain
                                                                                 properties, this method also eliminates boundary difficulties by keep­
                                                                                 ing the windows narrow. Moving average smoothing (moving window)

                                                                                     3.4. Lower limb classification (LLC)

                                                                                        The lower limb classification phase was performed using three deep
                                                                                     feedforward networks; RNN, LSTM, and GRU. The three deep network
                                                                                     models are suitable for processing the sequence data in terms of the
                                                                                     sEMG signal for the knee. Inherent ability to learn sequentially for the
                                                                                     three comparative time series deep networks; timestep was measured.
                                                                                     The three comparative deep networks; have the inherent ability to learn
                                                                                     sequentially from time series data.
                       Fig. 9. The Output Rectified Signal.




                                                Fig. 10. Concatenated and labeled data for each of the five muscles.

                                                                                 8
M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                                  Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391




                                                              Fig. 11. Balanced training data.


3.4.1. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs)                                           track Ct denotes carry, and the values at different timesteps are called Ct
    The RNN input sequence is represented in vectors. The vectors‘ size is        [33]. This data is integrated with the input and recurrent connections
determined by the timestep and input features. It iteratively cycles              via a dot product with a weight matrix, bias, and the use of an activation
through timesteps, combining its current state and the size input fea­            function. With the help of a multiplication operation and an activation
tures to produce the output at each timestep. The state of the RNN is             function, this will affect the state that is passed on to the next timestep.
reset between the processing of two independent sequences, so one                 The carry dataflow is a mechanism to modify the following output and
sequence is still considered a single input to the network. This data point       state from a conceptual standpoint, as seen next. Fig. 13 depicts the
is handled in a single step instead of looping the network over the               anatomy of an LSTM [11].
sequence elements, as shown in Fig. 12 [32].                                          This is what the interpretation of these procedures is supposed to
    In our case, the final result is a 2D vector of timesteps and output          accomplish. Multiplying ct and ft , for example, is a method of erasing
features, with each timestep representing the loop’s current output. The          irrelevant data from the carried data flow. In the meantime, it , and ot ,
following overview of the whole state sequence is obtained by training a          give current information, updating the carry track with new data [14].
basic recurrent network with an embedding layer and standard RNN
layer, as shown in Algorithm 4.




                                                                                  3.4.3. Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) redundant
                                                                                     Using SGD and early stopping-based classification loss, the networks
3.4.2. Long Short-Term memory (LSTM) (Redundant)                                  are trained. Early stopping during data training reduces the models
   LSTM is characterized by additional data flows of information across           overfitting, although hyper-parameter adjustment is also necessary to
timesteps for representing the difficult case that occurred via a carry           get a suitable local solution [13]. The network architecture’s size,


                                                                              9
M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                                   Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391




                                                       Fig. 12. The entire sequence of basic RNN.




                                                            Fig. 13. The anatomy of LSTM.


number of layers, learning rate, batch size, momentum, and sequence              Table 1
length are the hyper-parameters of importance. Trials or stochastic              An overview of the Lower Limb dataset.
searches are typically used to set tuning hyper-parameters. It is recom­          Segment           Lower Limb
mended over manual search since LSTM networks are resource-inten­
                                                                                  Channel           Ch1          Ch2            Ch3            Ch4           Ch5
sive. While other parameters are set to standard settings, the                    Muscle            RF           BF             VM             ST            FX
architecture and learning parameters need user adjustment.                        Column            0            1              2              3             4


4. Results and discussion
                                                                                 a sitting posture, and walking. The data was collected using four elec­
                                                                                 trodes (Vastus Medialis, Biceps Femoris, Rectus Femoris, and Semite­
   This section introduces the dataset that was utilised, the performance
                                                                                 ndinosus) and a knee goniometer. The total number of electrodes is four,
indicators, and the algorithm’s outcomes.
                                                                                 one for each channel in the time series (1–4). Each subject has five shares
                                                                                 or motion repeats in each series. Each data file has five columns, orga­
4.1. Dataset description                                                         nized as shown in Table 1.
                                                                                     The segment denotes the location on the body where the data is
   This database contains samples from 11 healthy people and 11 who              collected, and the channel denotes the electrode linked to a muscle.
had a knee abnormality diagnosed by a specialist [34]. Electromyog­              Recto Femoral (RF), Semitendinosus (ST), Vastus Medialis (VM), Biceps
raphy and goniometry equipment, as well as MWX8 Datalog Biometrics,              Femoral (BF), and Flexion (FX) of the Knee are all abbreviated as RF, BF,
were used to get the data. Eight digital and four analog channels were           VM, ST, and FX, as shown in Table 2. Each subject is photographed in
employed by the MWX8 data log, with four for sampling and one for                three ways: sitting, standing, and walking. The training database has 66
goniometry. Using a microSD card, this data was directly stored on the           records for knee abnormalities along with normal ones, with five attri­
computer’s internal storage and then sent through Bluetooth to software          butes describing the corresponding muscles measured. The dataset was
with real-time datalogging with a 14-bit resolution and a 1000 Hz                augmented using a time-series generator that yielded samples and their
sample rate.                                                                     targets. The augmentation step resulted in 1056 records of EMG
   The 22 participants in our study underwent three actions to examine           sequence signals for the five corresponding muscles.
behaviour related to the knee muscle: leg flexion up, leg extension from

M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                            Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391


Table 2
Normal and Knee Abnormality samples for five core muscles.




4.2. Performance metrics                                                      • ACC is calculated as the percentage of accurate predictions for all
                                                                                samples by Equation (4) [35]. The ACC is determined by the
   The model’s performance is evaluated via four different performance          following equation in terms of these arguments, true positive (TP),
indicators using a 10-fold cross-validation technique. The performance          false positive (FP), true negative (TN), and false negative (FN) [36].
indicators are ACC, Loss, Validation Acc, and Validation Loss.                  TP shows that the predicted cases and their actual output are true,


                                                                         11
M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                                       Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391


    while TN demonstrates the falsity of the expected cases and their real             dimensional area beneath the entire ROC curve, from (0,0) to (1,1).
    output. The predicted cases are confirmed by FP, but the actual                    AUC provides a comprehensive evaluation of the model’s perfor­
    output is demonstrated to be erroneous. Finally, FN demonstrates                   mance across all classification thresholds. It can be interpreted as the
    that while the actual output is correct, the predicted cases are false.            probability that the model will rank a random positive case higher
    Arguments are calculated for knee abnormalities against normal ones                than a random negative case [16].
    [37].

ACC =
               TP + TN
                                                                        (4)         4.3. Experimental results
          TP + TN + FP + FN
                                                                                        The proposed framework is implemented using MATLAB 2017 on an
                                                                                    HP Envy laptop with an AMD FX CPU for concatenation and shuffling.
                                                                                    Cloud computing (Google Colab) is used for augmentation and classifi­
 • The loss function is a measure of model prediction in terms of pre­              cation steps, and Colab is a GPU-centric application for accelerating
   dicting the expected outcomes. It maps the values of one or more                 deep learning. The runtime hardware configuration to execute the
   variables onto a real case associated with the study. It includes                model was 12 GB of RAM, the Nvidia K80 GPU, and 2496 CUDA cores.
   various terms around the difference between determined and real-                     Four performance measures, accuracy, loss, validation accuracy,
   world values for data samples. The objective is either to minimize               validation loss, recall, precision, and F1-score are used to evaluate the
   a loss function or its opposite, which is to be maximized [38]. The              model’s performance via 10-fold cross-validation. A comparative
   loss function can be calculated based on a quadratic function,                   experiment is performed for the three deep learning networks for time
   commonly called the Mean Squared Error (MSE) technique. The                      series and sequence data. The commonly used deep learning techniques
   quadratic function is particularly good because of its variance                  for time series and sequence data are simple RNN, LSTM, and GRU. The
   properties and because it is symmetric—the error above the target                deep networks are suitable for input data sequences regarding sEMG
   results in a loss value equivalent to the error below the target. If the         sequence signals for the corresponding five muscles. For continuous
   target is t, then a quadratic loss function f(x) is given by Equation (5)        estimation of the knee signal, the utilized deep techniques process the
   [36].                                                                            sEMG input sequences by iterating through the sequence elements and
f(x) = c ( t − x)2                                                      (5)         maintaining a state that contains information about what they have
                                                                                    seen. The summary of the results in terms of ACC and loss is given in
where C is a constant, that makes no difference and can be ignored by               Table 3 and Table 4 for each of the five muscles, RF, BF, VM, ST, and FX,
setting its value to 1.
    The loss function can also be determined based on the cross-entropy.            Table 3
They both compute the difference between the probability that occurs                The performance evaluation for accuracy for the three comparative deep
and the probability that is predicted. Cross-entropy loss employs nega­             networks.
tive log probability for the outputs, whereas cross-entropy still uses log           Recurrent Net       Accuracy
probability, as given by Equation (6) [39].                                                              RF           BF            VM            ST            FX
f(x) = − log(p(x))                                                      (6)          Simple RNN          0.961        0.951         0.915         0.938         0.834

    A softmax function can be used to easily convert log probabilities               GRU                 0.976        0.963         0.945         0.945         0.857
into normal numbers for computation. When using cross-entropy loss
instead of mean squared error loss, models with sigmoid and softmax
outputs perform better than those that suffer from saturation and poor              Table 4
learning. The forecast of a short-term vs. long-term prediction model is            The performance evaluation for loss for the three comparative deep networks.
contrasted in time series classification problems using cross-entropy                Recurrent Net       Loss
loss. When one class has a significantly higher probability, cross-                                      RF           BF            VM            ST            FX
entropy loss results in a significant penalty [40].

                                                                                     LSTM                0.147        0.176         0.264         0.157         0.508
 • The F1 score is a metric used to evaluate the accuracy of a test. It              GRU                 0.111        0.157         0.218         0.288         0.461
   takes into account both precision and recall values obtained from the
   test. Precision measures the proportion of true positive results among
   all positive predictions, while recall measures the proportion of true           Table 5
   positive results among all actual positive samples. The F1 score is              The performance evaluation for validation accuracy for the three comparative
                                                                                    deep networks.
   calculated as given by Equation (7) [16]:
                                                                                     Recurrent Net       Validation Accuracy
               2 x TP
F1 =                                                                    (7)                              RF           BF            VM            ST            FX
        (2 x TP + FP + FN)
                                                                                     Simple RNN          0.943        0.934         0.897         0.934         0.687
                                                                                     LSTM                0.945        0.941         0.903         0.931         0.775
 • Precision, which is also known as positive predictive value, is a                 GRU                 0.949        0.944         0.939         0.936         0.787
   metric used to assess the accuracy of a test. It is calculated by
   dividing the number of true positive results by the total number of
                                                                                    Table 6
   positive predictions, including false positives. The formula for pre­
                                                                                    The performance evaluation for validation loss for the three comparative deep
   cision is given by Equation (8) [1]:

               (TP + FP)

 • AUC stands for “Area Under the ROC Curve”. It is a metric that                    LSTM                0.217        0.220         0.175         0.170         0.631
   quantifies the overall performance of a model by measuring the two-               GRU                 0.212        0.216         0.179         0.135         0.687

Fine-tuning the hyperparameters in training the three comparative deep networks.
  Recurrent Net          Hyperparameters

                         Embedding              LSTM           Activation             Optimizer           Loss                            Epochs            Batch size          Validation split

  Simple RNN             (1000, 32)             32             ’sigmoid’              ’rmsprop’           ’binary_crossentropy’           30                128                 0.2

                                                                                                 13
M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                                  Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391


                                                                                  Simple RNN model performs moderately well, with a validation accu­
                                                                                  racy ranging from 0.687 to 0.943 depending on the variant. The models
                                                                                  by Ibraheem et al. and Bansal et al. perform less well, with validation
                                                                                  accuracies ranging from 0.837 to 0.858. These results suggest that the
                                                                                  use of RNN models, particularly GRU and LSTM, can be effective for this
                                                                                  task, and that careful selection of the specific RNN architecture can have
                                                                                  a significant impact on performance.

                                                                                  4.4. Discussion

                                                                                      The three deep learning networks we used in this paper look like
                                                                                  feedforward neural networks, with one difference: they have connec­
                                                                                  tions pointing backward. The recurrent layer gets the input x(t) and the
                                                                                  output from the previous time step at each time step t. Each recurrent
                                                                                  neuron in the network has two weight sets, one for the input at the
                                                                                  present time step (wx) and the other for the previous time’s outputs. This
                                                                                  time series technique somehow replaced the need for a feature extrac­
    Fig. 15. The training and validation loss curves over epochs for RNN.
                                                                                  tion step, saving time and effort. As mentioned previously, the proposed
                                                                                  technique’s primary goal is to diagnose knee abnormalities based on
                                                                                  analyzing the sEMG signals. The system is based on the idea that deep
                                                                                  learning techniques can automatically extract features from the given
                                                                                  data. We used two deep learning techniques: RNN, LSTM, and GRU, to
                                                                                  compare the results and use the one with the best results. We tuned the
                                                                                  parameter settings to achieve the best accuracy. The parameters we used
                                                                                  are listed in Table 8.
                                                                                      The experimental results show that GRU gives us the best results. On
                                                                                  the other hand, we applied an electrode to five different muscles in the
                                                                                  knee, and the extracted signals were detected to be used as input to the
                                                                                  deep learning network. The tables above show the lower limb deep
                                                                                  model classification accuracy and loss using different time series and
                                                                                  deep sequence networks for sEMG for each of the five knee muscles
                                                                                  using 10-fold cross-validation. Tables 3, 4, and 5 show that the best
                                                                                  results are obtained using the GRU network and show higher results with
                                                                                  the signals from RF, RF, BF, VM, ST and FX, respectively. Table 6 shows
                                                                                  that the best results are obtained using the GRU network and show
                                                                                  higher results with the signals from FX, ST, BF, VM, RF and BF,
    Fig. 16. The training and validation loss curves over epochs for LSTM.        respectively.
                                                                                      The results of the comparative study show that the GRU sequence
                                                                                  deep network model outperforms other time series deep network tech­
                                                                                  niques. RNN is commonly used due to its simplicity in real-world ap­
                                                                                  plications. But, RNN needs to remember information about inputs
                                                                                  viewed many timesteps before; at time t, it is difficult to learn such long-
                                                                                  term dependencies in practise. Thus, due to the vanishing gradient
                                                                                  problem with many-layer non-recurrent networks (feedforward net­
                                                                                  works), the network becomes untrainable as the number of layers in the
                                                                                  network grows.
                                                                                      LSTM and GRU layers conquer this issue by stacking the data from
                                                                                  the grouping onto the transport line whenever it is conveyed to a later
                                                                                  timestep and, afterward, can be recovered depending on the situation.
                                                                                  The LSTM accomplishes this by preserving information for future use
                                                                                  and preventing the loss of older signals. GRU layer works on the same
                                                                                  premise as LSTM but is more streamlined and thus less expensive to run.
                                                                                  Table 7 confirms the ability of GRU to learn sequentially using min time
                                                                                  steps more than that for the three comparative time series deep net­
                                                                                  works; each timestep appears to have min values for the signals from
      Fig. 17. The training and validation loss curves over GRU epochs.           VM, BF, FX, and RF to ST, respectively.
                                                                                      For the calculation time comparison, Table 8 shows that RNN ach­
muscles, RF, BF, VM, ST, and FX, respectively.                                    ieves training in less time than LSTM and GRU as the number of layers in
   As illustrated in Table 13, it compares the performance results of             a network grows. LSTM and GRU layers in a neural network can store
various recurrent neural network (RNN) models for a given task, as                information from a sequence and pass it along to future time steps,
measured by validation accuracy. The models include Simple RNN,                   allowing for long-term dependencies to be captured, albeit at the cost of
LSTM, and GRU, as well as two previously published models by Ibra­                increased computational complexity. The training and validation loss
heem et al. and Bansal et al. The results indicate that the GRU model             curves are constructed to visualize the performance of the three
performs the best overall, with a validation accuracy of 0.949. The LSTM          comparative time-series deep network models. Fig. 15 displays the loss
model also performs well, with a validation accuracy of 0.945. The                curves for training and validation over epochs for an RNN. Fig. 16 dis­
                                                                                  plays the loss curves for training and validation over epochs for LSTM.

                                                                             14
M.R. Ibraheem et al.                                                                                                                       Egyptian Informatics Journal 24 (2023) 100391

                                                                                                      model – A deep learning approach for medical data with irregularity in cancer
GRU.
                                                                                                      predication with tumor markers. Comput Biol Med May 2022;144:105362.
    The comparison of the lower limb GRU sequence deep network                                    [8] Sen S, Raghunathan A. Approximate computing for long short term memory
model with other sequence deep network models (RNN and LSTM)                                          (LSTM) neural networks. IEEE Trans Comput Aided Des Integr Circuits Syst Nov.
demonstrated its promising results. Fig. 17 illustrates that the evaluation                           2018;37:2266–76.

score consistently outperforms the scores obtained with RNN and LSTM                                  methodology for bike sharing availability prediction. Procedia Comput Sci 2019;

5. Conclusion                                                                                    [11] Hu J, Zheng W, “Transformation-gated LSTM: efficient capture of short-term
                                                                                                      mutation dependencies for multivariate time series prediction tasks”, arXiv:
    A non-invasive time-sequence model has been proposed to accu­                                     1907.03152, July 2019.
                                                                                                 [12] Gao S, Huang Y, Zhang S, Han J, Wang G, Zhang M, et al. Short-term runoff
rately characterize muscle activity based on sEMG signals. The proposed                               prediction with GRU and LSTM networks without requiring time step optimization
deep learning model is designed to handle time-sequential data by                                     during sample generation. J Hydrol 2020;589:125188.
controlling the input flow and avoiding the vanishing gradient problem.                          [13] Li Q, Xu Y. VS-GRU: a variable sensitive gated recurrent neural network for
                                                                                                      multivariate time series with massive missing values. Appl Sci 2019;9:3041.
Three feedforward deep learning networks - RNN, LSTM, and GRU -                                  [14] Mou L, Zhao P, Xie H, Chen Y. T-LSTM: a long short-term memory neural network
have been used to represent and classify time series and sequence data.                               enhanced by temporal information for traffic flow prediction. IEEE Access 2019;7:
While RNN struggles with learning long-term dependencies, LSTM and                                    98053–60.
                                                                                                 [15] Ibraheem MR. Lower limb analysis based on surface electromyography (semg)
GRU layers can retain information from the sequence at any time and                                   using different time-frequency representation techniques. Int J Adv Sci Eng
retrieve it later. GRU layers have fewer parameters, superior perfor­                                 Information Technol 2023;13(1):24–33.
mance, and consume less memory than LSTM layers, making them faster                              [16] Bansal H, Chinagundi B, Rana PS, Kumar N. An ensemble machine learning
                                                                                                      technique for detection of abnormalities in knee movement sustainability.
and more efficient in practice. For larger datasets and lengthy sequences,                            Sustainability Oct. 2022;14(20):13464. https://doi.org/10.3390/su142013464.
